entry.title: Trading partner determination for PGI Material accounting document during intercompany transaction
entry.content:
I have created an intercompany sales order for an end customer as the material is sourced from a different plant.

During PGI of delivery, we are getting issue related to invalid trading partner.

In I/C sales order, we have sold-to party ABC, sales org 1234, delivering plant 5678.

We have maintained config in OVVA (internal customer for sales org intercompany transactions)

When we post PGI for the delivery, it is determining the trading in material accounting doc which is related to a different sales org (ex: 3456). How do we check how the customer OR trading partner is being determined for the inventory accounting (PGI) document between customer 1234 (for sales org 1234) & supplier 5678 (delivering plant 5678).

entry.title: Re: How can I deploy Joule Skills / Agents from Studio to S/4HANA's Joule Bot?
entry.content:
When setting up Joule Studio through Joule Booster or by updating a Formation, you need to add all the systems connected to Joule S4, Ariba, or others.

After that, in Joule Studio, create an environment, share the environment capabilities, and deploy any project to this environment.

Please explore how to make an environment a shared one: https://help.sap.com/docs/Joule_Studio/45f9d2b8914b4f0ba731570ff9a85313/6b0a25c11bc54daf84c29a5f9b82c87d.html?locale=en-US

entry.title: SE78: “Logo not found” error when sending mail with inline image
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

I am facing an issue while sending emails that include an inline logo image.

During the email sending process, the system throws a “Logo not found” error related to SE78, and the email is not sent.

What I Have Checked

  • Verified that the logo exists in SE78

  • Confirmed that the logo name is correctly referenced in the program

  • Checked SCOT configuration

  • SMTP is working correctly

  • Executed RSCONN01 manually

Despite these checks, the error persists.

Question

Are there additional settings or dependencies in SE78 that could cause this issue?

Is there anything specific I should verify regarding logo storage type (BMAP/BDS) or client dependency?

 

The code block is below.

REPORT zsg_html_mail_with_logo.

DATA: lo_bcs       TYPE REF TO cl_bcs,
      lo_document  TYPE REF TO cl_document_bcs,
      lo_recipient TYPE REF TO if_recipient_bcs.

DATA: lt_html TYPE soli_tab.

DATA: lt_bds_content TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF bapiconten,
      lt_content_hex TYPE solix_tab,
      lv_xstring     TYPE xstring,
      lv_size        TYPE so_obj_len,
      lv_input_len   TYPE i.

CALL FUNCTION 'SAPSCRIPT_GET_GRAPHIC_BDS'
  EXPORTING
    i_object  = 'GRAPHICS'
    i_name    = 'HTML_MAIL_LOGO'     
    i_id      = 'BMAP'
    i_btype   = 'BCOL'
  TABLES
    content   = lt_bds_content
  EXCEPTIONS
    not_found = 1
    OTHERS    = 2.

IF sy-subrc <> 0.
  MESSAGE 'Logo not found in SE78' TYPE 'E'.
ENDIF.

lv_input_len = lines( lt_bds_content ) * 255.

CALL FUNCTION 'SCMS_BINARY_TO_XSTRING'
  EXPORTING
    input_length = lv_input_len
  IMPORTING
    buffer       = lv_xstring
  TABLES
    binary_tab   = lt_bds_content
  EXCEPTIONS
    failed       = 1
    OTHERS       = 2.

IF sy-subrc <> 0.
  MESSAGE 'Binary to XSTRING conversion failed' TYPE 'E'.
ENDIF.

CALL FUNCTION 'SCMS_XSTRING_TO_BINARY'
  EXPORTING
    buffer     = lv_xstring
  TABLES
    binary_tab = lt_content_hex.

lv_size = xstrlen( lv_xstring ).

APPEND '<html><body>' TO lt_html.
APPEND '<h2>HTML Mail Test</h2>' TO lt_html.
APPEND '<img src="cid:logo_cid">' TO lt_html.
APPEND '<p>This email was sent via SAP ABAP using CL_BCS.</p>' TO lt_html.
APPEND '</body></html>' TO lt_html.

lo_bcs = cl_bcs=>create_persistent( ).

lo_document = cl_document_bcs=>create_document(
  i_type    = 'HTM'
  i_text    = lt_html
  i_subject = 'Inline Image HTML Mail Test' ).

lo_bcs->set_document( lo_document ).

lo_document->add_attachment(
  i_attachment_type    = 'PNG'
  i_attachment_subject = 'Logo'
  i_attachment_size    = lv_size
  i_att_content_hex    = lt_content_hex
  i_attachment_header  = VALUE soli_tab(
                           ( line = 'Content-ID: <logo_cid>' )
                           ( line = 'Content-Disposition: inline' ) ) ).

lo_recipient =
  cl_cam_address_bcs=>create_internet_address( 'xxxx.yyyy@gmail.com' ).

lo_bcs->add_recipient( lo_recipient ).

lo_bcs->send( ).

COMMIT WORK.

WRITE: 'Mail successfully sent.'.

entry.title: Re: Employee Time Off Request auto approval before Payrol...
entry.content:
Hi @rsriramm ,

This is quite an interesting requirement, and although I wouldn’t call myself a CPI expert, there is a logical flow you can follow to build a robust and automated solution. Think of it as a three‑step journey: first identifying the right workflow requests, then changing the approver, and finally approving them through the API.

Step 1 — Identifying the Workflow Requests

Our journey begins by figuring out which workflow requests actually meet your criteria.
To do this, I would create the initial integration flow to retrieve all employee time records where the end date is on or before the payroll cut‑off date and pending for approval.

You can control this dynamically by scheduling the job to run every 14 days and filtering the records using today’s date (end date on or before today). Since each employee time record includes the field workflowRequestId, this becomes the key piece of data we need. Once identified, store those workflow request IDs in a data store so they can be processed in the next steps.

Step 2 — Reassigning the Approver to the API User

Before the system can approve any workflow automatically, the approval rights need to be aligned. Since a workflow can only be approved by its designated approver, the next step is to change the approver to your API user.

Using the workflow request IDs stored earlier, you can trigger changeWfRequestApprover to update the approver accordingly.

Step 3 — Approving the Workflow Requests

Now that the API user is officially the approver, the final step becomes straightforward.
Using the same dataset from your data store, call the approveWfRequest to complete the workflow approvals automatically.

 
I hope this gives you a solid structure to build your solution.

Regards,
VR

entry.title: Re: check is not getting assigned in public cloud
entry.content:
Dear @muhammad_yousufali,

If neither Manage Automatic Payments (F0770) nor Create Single Payment (F0743) assigns a check number, then in Public Cloud it typically means the system cannot determine a valid checkbook/check lot for payment method C most commonly due to an inconsistent / multiple-chain checkbook setup or an exhausted check lot. Output Parameter Determination only affects printing after the check exists it does not create the check number

SAP Notes below for this situation that I sent in my first answer:
• https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/2706870
• https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3605460
• https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3352922

The quickest way to confirm which case you hit is the exact error text in the payment run log / message because it usually matches one of the above SAP Notes directly

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: SAP Automation Pilot – Empowering IT Operations with AI Insights
entry.content:
I am very happy to share this interesting scenario to you inspired by the powerful collaboration between Johnson Controls International (JCI), the SAP MaxAttention, and the SAP Automation Pilot team, showcasing how automation and AI are reshaping enterprise IT operations. Kudos to everyone from the teams involved! 

Note: a link to the public Customer Story here: JCI: Building a global foundation for smart growth by strengthening IT reliability and efficiency

The Challenge: Monitoring Complexity at Scale

JCI operates a large and complex SAP ERP landscape — spanning more than 30 SAP Enterprise Management systems. For an enterprise company — system uptime and reliability are non-negotiable.

Until recently, JCI relied on manual monitoring, with IT staff logging in every six hours to track KPIs, collect system data, and prepare reports. This process was time-consuming, repetitive, and resource-intensive, often resulting in outdated data and increased risk of unplanned downtime.

The Solution: AI-Powered Automation with SAP Automation Pilot

To tackle this challenge, JCI turned to SAP Automation Pilot integrated with SAP AI Core service. The team implemented an innovative solution that automates the collection of system health KPIs and generates intelligent insights using generative AI — all within a low-code environment.

This setup replaced thousands of hours of manual work with an automated, intelligent monitoring process across JCI’s SAP landscape. The automation not only removed the risk of missed issues but also ensured 24/7 visibility into system performance and health.

Working closely with the SAP MaxAttention and SAP Automation Pilot team,  JCI gained the benefit of guidance, innovation discovery workshops, hands-on architectural and implementation support, helping transform vision into a fully functional, scalable pilot.

“SAP Automation Pilot enables automated collection of system health KPIs and generates insights using generative AI in a low-code environment,” shared Chandra Talasila from JCI team. 

See below an example of a reference automation flow modelled and run in SAP Automation Pilot to automated systems' health status  AI-powered insights. 

The Business Impact: Continuity and Efficiency

The results were immediate and tangible, see below:

  • 3,000+ hours saved annually through automation
  • Significant cost savings and productivity gains
  • Elimination of unplanned downtime risk, ensuring continuity across manufacturing and operations

This shift has freed up IT teams to focus on high-value work, transforming system monitoring from a reactive task into a strategic capability that supports proactive decision-making and operational resilience.

Looking Ahead: Expanding AI and Automation

Building on this successful foundation, JCI is now accelerating its AI and automation roadmap. Future plans include:

  • Automating pre- and post-maintenance validations
  • Streamlining recurring IT tickets
  • Deploying digital tools for outage communication management
  • Exploring SAP’s Business AI offerings, including SAP Joule and SAP EarlyWatch Alert, for broader integration and insight delivery

Looking ahead, SAP MaxAttention and SAP Automation Pilot will continue to play a key role in supporting these forward-looking plans—empowering SAP customers to embrace AI, accelerate innovation, and bring tangible value in their day-to-day operations. As AI becomes an integral part of enterprise IT strategy, our commitment remains strong: to partner with customers at every step, enabling smarter and more resilient business outcomes.

In case you have any comments and / or ideas for other automation scenarios - please do not hesitate to leave a comment or reach out directly at biser.simeonov@sap.com

BDC Connect enables a secure, bidirectional zero-copy sharing of data products by preserving valuable semantics and business context between SAP BDC and partner systems like Databricks, Google, Microsoft or Snowflake.

SAP Databricks is using a similar technology out of the box of BDC.

entry.title: Embedded Analytics - Custom Cube - Calculated Key Figure shows no data
entry.content:
Hello Experts,

let me give you some additional information before explaining the issue.

Back in R/3 we used KE35 (Form) and KER1 (Key Figure Scheme) to prepare for KE30 (Profitability Report) in SAP CO-PA modul.

So far i have done significant tests only with transient view entity … analytical query and was able to replace our custom reports from R/3 costing based COPA to S/4 Margin Analysis queries and the results are very good.

However, i noticed that using a custom consumption view offers very little reuseability. A new requirement triggers a new consumption view and copy/paste of the CDS view. So i want to achieve REUSEABILITY for restricted measures and calculated measures. Looking at the SAP VDM i think it is time to build custom cubes, which i successfully circumvented so far.

So how can i replace R/3 „Forms“, „Key Figures Schemes“ and „Reports“ with Embedded Analytics? I am trying to do a crossover to the VDM which uses cubes, querys, restricted measures, and calculated measures. (Screenshot shows R/3 setup of CO-PA reporting)

 

 

I am not trying to replicate the old world 1 by 1, but take over what is still useful e.g. „Prepare a Form“ and „Prepare Key Figures“ and reuse this in multiple querys.

I created the above „custom VDM“ and while the values of the restricted measures are shown correct (see below, RSRT) in the consumption view, the calcuated measures are always inital. What am i doing wrong?

Formulas are very simple:

Revenue - COGS = ContributionMargin1 and FIDirect - OverhAlloc = FIMinusOverhead

My guess is that the calculated measures may have lost their dimensions? Could that be the reason?

Or do i not understand how aggregation works?

Greatful for any tipps. I can share the CDS Views if needed.

Thank you

Regards

David

entry.title: Calculated Measures show no data - Embedded Analytics

Hello Experts,

let me explain the background first.

Back in R/3 we used KE35 (Form) and KER1 (Key Figure Scheme) to prepare for KE30 (Profitability Report).

So far i have done significant tests only with transient view entity … analytical query and was able to replace our custom reports from R/3 costing based COPA to S/4 Margin Analysis queries and the results are very good.

entry.title: Re: Trying reconcile CO09 with BAPI_MATERIAL_AVAILABILITY
entry.content:
Leo,

In CO09, the quantities returned by BAPI_MATERIAL_AVAILABILITY (880 and 100) correspond to the cumulative ATP (**bleep**. ATP) at batch level on the requested date.

 

Where to point on the CO09 screen:

1. Batch MIXTEST1 → 880

Look at the lines:

  • MRP element = BtsLoc
  • Stock segment = MIXTEST1 / FG
  • On the requested date (02/13/2026)
  • Check the “**bleep**. ATP” column

 

You will see 880 as the cumulative available quantity. This is calculated as:

  • Total stock (973)
  • Minus open requirements (132 – 60 = 72)
  • Resulting ATP = 880

 

That 880 is exactly what the BAPI returns in COM_QTY.

 

2. Batch 04221901 → 100

 

Look at:

  • MRP element = BtsLoc
  • Stock segment = 04221901 / FG
  • Same requested date
  • In the “**bleep**. ATP” column

 

 

You’ll see 100, which matches the BAPI COM_QTY.

There are no consuming requirements against this batch, so ATP = available stock = 100.

 

Important things I learn from my experience BAPI_MATERIAL_AVAILABILITY does not return the raw stock quantity.

It returns the confirmed ATP quantity as of the requested date, which in CO09 is reflected in the “**bleep**. ATP” column at the batch (BtsLoc) level.

entry.title: AI’s Role in Organizational Change Management
entry.content:
Let's be honest: most change managers spend more time maintaining spreadsheets than having the conversations that matter or leveraging their empathy and personal skills. AI won't replace OCM—but it can handle the pattern-heavy work, freeing you for strategy, coaching, and navigating organizational dynamics. This post explores how AI augments the six core OCM dimensions in SAP transformations, based on our real project experience.

As part of the OCM Digital Change and Adoption team within SAP’s Consulting Services organization, we lead SAP Organizational Change Management in various  SAP transformations. We recognize that Artificial Intelligence (AI) is reshaping also our work —introducing new opportunities and challenges across how we plan, lead, and sustain change. This post explores how AI could influence our Organizational Change Management (OCM) approach in SAP transformations and beyond.

Importantly, these are practitioner perspectives—not product announcements. Our intent is to share how we see the six OCM dimensions evolving, based on our project experience. To keep the focus on opportunities, we deliberately set aside topics such as GDPR[1] and AI ethics here—though they remain essential considerations in practice.

As OCM practitioners, we advise our customers throughout their transformation journeys. Our approach is aligned to the SAP Activate methodology and addresses six core dimensions, underpinned by data-driven solutions.

Below, you’ll find each dimension, how we apply it today, and how AI could augment it. For clarity, we’ve included a graphic of the six OCM dimensions here.

 

Impact key:

  • 🟩 Medium: Broader acceleration and decision support
  • 🟦 Medium to High: Significant scaling and personalization potential
  • 🟪 High: Step-change in effectiveness and proactivity

 

🟦 Change Strategy sets direction, aligns stakeholders, and prioritizes the people side of the transformation.
Today: We tailor the change approach to your program context, define scope and outcomes, map stakeholders, and plan governance, milestones, and enablement strategies using SAP good practices. 
With AI: We auto‑draft change strategies, stakeholder snapshots, and governance calendars from your business case, project charter, and discovery notes; synthesize interview transcripts into themes and risks; and run scenario comparisons to test plan options. AI also generates first‑cut decks and exec summaries that consultants refine with context.
Impact: Faster strategy and planning cycles, clearer stakeholder alignment early on, and fewer mid‑stream rework loops.

🟩 Change Leadership helps sponsors and managers lead with clarity, consistency, and credibility.

Today: We align leadership on vision and roles, map stakeholders, and equip sponsors with engagement plans, talking points, and routines that sustain momentum.
With AI: We analyze stakeholder networks and sentiment to identify influencers and resistance patterns, recommend an optimal change network for your context, and generate sponsor briefings tailored by audience and region. AI also suggests targeted outreach sequences and tracks follow‑through.
Impact: Faster stakeholder mapping, higher sponsor visibility and touchpoints, earlier detection of at‑risk groups. Which can improve confidence and speed of leadership action.

🟪 Change Communication helps you keep stakeholders informed, engaged, and aligned throughout the transformation.

Today: We craft communication plans, key messages, and stakeholder-specific content based on persona analysis and project milestones.
With AI: We can generate personalized newsletters, FAQs, and leadership talking points in multiple languages and formats, whatever fits the project. E.e. leverage transcripts of AMA Sessions to create a blog in your sharepoint incl a Q&A list.  AI analyzes and summarizes sentiment from your employee feedback channels to adjust messaging before resistance escalates.
Impact: Faster and more personalized content creation, higher engagement rates, real-time neutral sentiment tracking.

🟩 Change Realization turns strategy into targeted actions that move people from current to future ways of working.

Today: We assess process, role, and location impacts; prioritize change measures; and coordinate workshops to translate findings into concrete action plans.
With AI: We accelerate impact analysis from design assets and process maps, cluster similar impacts across your units, and auto‑draft tailored action plans and playbooks fitting for your project—reducing reliance on lengthy manual workshops while preserving expert review.
Impact: Faster impact‑to‑action cycle, fewer workshop hours, clearer prioritization across roles and regions. So that the projects change activities translate into visible progress sooner.

🟦 Change Enablement equips teams and end users with the skills and confidence to adopt new solutions and processes.

Today: We design learning strategies, build curricula, and deliver role‑based training, job aids, and hypercare support aligned to milestones.
With AI: We generate role‑specific learning paths, adapt content to proficiency and language, create just‑in‑time guidance, and use usage data to recommend next‑best learning, tailored to your needs, for this you can use SAP’s Learning Needs Analysis (LNA) microapp. AI generates drafts automatic from sources such as Signavio, SAP Best Practices or CALM.  In the flow of work, you can combine this with WalkMe Learning Arc (where available) to deliver in‑app, role‑aware walkthroughs and micro‑learning that reinforce new behaviors at the point of need. AI also drafts quizzes, simulations, demos, exercises and scenario‑based practice from source materials and managed via Prompts.
Impact: Faster content creation, easier content updates, higher course completion and satisfaction, reduced time‑to‑competence post go‑live. This can improve the adoption where it matters most in the project.

🟪 Change Effectiveness ensures change activities deliver outcomes and remain sustainable over time.

Today: We define KPIs, collect feedback, run health checks, and apply corrective actions based on adoption, performance, and sentiment trends.
With AI: We combine digital adoption checks, benchmarks, and pattern detection to spot emerging risks, forecast slippage, and recommend targeted interventions before issues escalate. AI evaluates and summarizes your feedback channels and explains variance across different sites or roles in your company.
Impact: Risks identified several weeks earlier before they impact your project value, fewer late‑stage escalations, continuous, fact based adoption insights with numbers.

With these six dimensions, you can transition from the status quo to future-ready ways of working more smoothly —so you can realize the full value of your SAP solutions. AI doesn’t replace the human side of change; it amplifies it. It can free consultants and customers to focus on higher-value activities: judgement, alignment, personalization and leadership.

This post reflects our experience and practitioner view of where AI can deliver value today.Agentic AI might even bring more oppportunities.While we intentionally focus on possibilities here, topics such as GDPR, security, and responsible AI are non‑negotiable in delivery and must be addressed in any implementation.

In our next blog post, we’ll dive deeper into the AI × OCM opportunities from a change manager’s perspective, sharing concrete use cases and practical recommendations.

Let us know what you think—we’d love to hear your perspective and experiences.

[1] GDPR: General Data Protection Regulation (DSGVO in German)

entry.title: Expanding Visual Analysis: New Area Chart, Pareto Chart, and Stacked Area Enhancements
entry.content:
As analytics adoption grows, Story Designers are increasingly expected to move beyond static reporting and deliver visual analysis that explains trends, highlights drivers, and supports prioritization.

While SAP Analytics Cloud already offers a strong foundation of visualizations, designers often faced limitations when trying to identify key contributors behind a metric, show how values evolve over time without visual clutter, or clearly communicate proportional impact and cumulative effects.

With the 2026 Q1 QRC, SAP Analytics Cloud expands its out-of-the-box visualization capabilities with new and enhanced charts: Pareto Chart, Area Chart, and improvements to the Stacked Area!

Pareto Chart: Focus on What Truly Drives Impact

The Pareto Chart is designed to help Story Designers to prioritize focus, explain trade-offs, and guide action instead of treating all contributors as equally important.

We wanted the creation of a Pareto Chart to be intentionally simple. Story Designers just have to bind a primary structure (Measure or Account depending on the Model Type) and select the dimension across which the analysis should run. The Pareto Visualization is then automatically generated, sorted from highest to lowest, including the cumulative percentage which increases to 100%. 

Furthermore, we allow additional Primary Structures as supplementary columns for context without impacting the Cumulative Percentage Calculation. It provides Story Designers the option to enrich the analysis while keeping the pareto logic clean and mathematically correct.

⚠️ For the Cumulative Percentage, Pareto Charts rely on a Running Total Calculation and therefore is not supported for data models that do not allow for the creation of a Running Total Calculation in Stories, such as SAP BW and SAP IBP. 

Area Chart: A Clear Alternative to Stacked Visuals

The new Area Chart introduces a powerful option for visualizing trends over time while maintaining focus on the overall shape and direction of the data. Compared to Stacked Area Charts, Area Charts emphasize trend and magnitude rather than composition, reduce visual complexity when multiple series are present, and make it easier to follow a single measure’s evolution across time. It makes Area Charts especially valuable when the analytical goal is trend interpretation rather than part-to-whole comparison.

Stacked Area Chart Enhancements: More Expressive, More Flexible

The Stacked Area Chart has been enhanced to support use cases that matter to you the most! These include the introduction of a 100% Stacked Area Chart, enabling designers to clearly visualize proportional contribution over time. We've also added the configuration to adjust the line to a smooth line, which also applies to the Area Chart and offers consistency with our other Chart Types. 

Why This Matters for Story Designers

These visualization enhancements significantly expand what Story Designers can do out of the box in SAP Analytics Cloud rather than relying on Custom Widgets.

As SAP Analytics Cloud continues to evolve its visualization portfolio, designers can rely less on workarounds and more on purpose-built visuals that match analytical intent.

ⓘ Keep an eye out on the roadmap explorer, as new Chart Types such as the Gauge, Funnel, and Sankey will be coming soon!

entry.title: Are you ready to share your HANA insights with the community?
entry.content:
Are you ready to share your HANA insights with our community?

HANA Tech Con will take place on July 16, and we’re looking for experts, users, and partners to delight us with engaging and inspiring sessions.

Ready to submit your proposal? Here is how:

1: Go to the HANA Tech Con website and click “Call for Proposals” on the main menu:

 

You can also find this option directly on the page by clicking on the button “Become a Speaker”:

2: Once you are inside the Call for Proposals page, you will find detailed information about the topics we are looking for.

💡Tip: We especially appreciate original, relevant, and engaging content.

 

3: Scroll to the end of the page and click the “Submit Your Talk” button:

 

4: Once you reach the submission page, you’ll need to choose a title for your Session:                 

 

Here are some examples from the first HANA Tech Con edition:

- SAP HANA HotSpots

- Mastering HANA Performance

- Database Analysis Using the SAP HANA Cloud Knowledge Graph Engine

- How to optimize the SAP HANA Memory Sizing Predictions

 

5: Select your Session Type based on:

  • TALK (Spot Talk or Deep Dive Talk);
  • PPTless Demo;
  • OTHER (If no format that aligns with your vision, just don’t forget to use the remarks field, not description, to describe your proposed session format). 
  • Extensive Talk.

The REMARKS FIELD is located just below the Description section:

 

6: Tell us about yourself!

If you’re presenting the session alone, please fill in your Name, E-mail and Company.

If your session includes another speaker, please click “Add Speaker” and complete their information as well. You can add up to eight speakers per session:

 

7: To submit your proposal, check the Consent Checkbox and click Submit Proposal.

 

MAKE SURE YOU HAVE SUBMITED YOUR PROPOSAL:

8: After submitting, you’ll see a confirmation page:

 

IMPORTANT INFORMATION:

  • Do NOT FORGET to verify your email, otherwise your proposal won’t be processed. You have 7 days after submitting your proposal to verify it.
  • Once verified, you will receive a link to view or edit your proposal.
  • You MAY submit more than one proposal.
  • Call for speakers closes at 11:59 pm on March 13, 2025.
  • Results will be communicated by email within 2–3 weeks after the deadline.

In case you have any questions, we’re here for you!

Contact us via info@hanatech.community.

And of course: Stay connected! We’re looking forward to seeing you on July 16.

Cheers,

HANA Tech Con Team

entry.title: Re: Implementing "Email Address" in your User Search
entry.content:
@heidirobinson Wonderful!  Glad to hear you are getting value out of it! 

My first guess at the issue would be that some of your "empty" email fields in fact, have white spaces.  Invisible to the eye, but even one space character would make the search selector consider it "not empty".

One way to check this would be to download the search results to CSV, and check it in Notepad (NOT Excel), or, if you have access to the archived connector input files), you could open tthose in NotePad as well.  

 

Excel has a tendancy to modify data, and hide spaces, so its not great for this type of check.

Chris

entry.title: The Architect’s Guide to SAP Fiscal Year Variants: Deciphering the Functional Mechanics of FYV.
entry.content:
Date: February 2026

Author:-  Er. Pratik Das (B.Tech, Mechanical Engineering , SAP Functional Consultant ).

Specialization :- MM, SCM Integration & Business Process Optimization.

Target Audience: SAP Functional Consultants (MM,FI), Solution Architects, Financial Controllers & Business Process Owners (BPO), SAP S/4HANA Migration Leads & etc.

In the SAP ecosystem, the Fiscal Year Variant (FYV) is far more than a simple calendar setting; it is the temporal foundation upon which all transactional integrity rests. For a Material Management (MM) Consultant, the FYV dictates the lifecycle of a Material Document, the calculation of moving average prices, and the strict boundaries of period-end closing via MMPV.

When we configure a system for a global rollout—balancing the Western (K4) and Indian (V3) statutory requirements—precision in OB29 is non-negotiable. Misconfiguration here doesn't just result in a ledger error; it can freeze a warehouse’s ability to post Goods Receipts (GR) or Issue Goods (GI).

  1. The Functional Mechanics of FYV :- 

The Fiscal Year Variant defines the number of posting periods in a fiscal year and the number of special periods. While the standard is 12 posting periods, SAP allows up to 16 periods in total.

  • Posting Periods: These correspond to the operational months where day-to-day procurement and inventory movements occur.
  • Special Periods (13–16): These are strictly for Finance (FI). As an MM consultant, it is vital to remember that logistics movements cannot be posted to special periods. These are reserved for audit adjustments, tax corrections, and year-end closing entries that do not affect physical inventory quantities.
  1. Calendar-Dependent vs. Year-Dependent: The Implementation Choice
  2. Calendar-Dependent (The "Set and Forget" Model)

Used predominantly in Western regions (Standard Variant K4), this configuration assumes the fiscal year is identical to the Gregorian calendar (January 1 to December 31).

  • Consultant Insight: When "Calendar Year" is flagged in OB29, the system ignores the "Period" and "Day" sub-tables. It automatically knows January has 31 days and is Period 01. This is the lowest-maintenance path for a Greenfield implementation.
  1. Year-Dependent (The High-Precision Model)

A variant is "Year-Dependent" if the start and end dates of the periods change every year.

  • The 4-4-5 Retail Cycle: Common in US/UK retail, where months are defined as cycles of weeks to ensure every quarter has exactly 13 weeks. Here, "Month 1" might end on January 28th one year and January 29th the next.
  • Configuration Necessity: Every year, the consultant or the power user must manually maintain the period end-dates for the upcoming year. Failure to do this before the clock strikes midnight on New Year's Eve will result in a global "Period not defined" hard error for every Purchase Order (PO) and GR.
  1. Geographical Logic: Western (K4) vs. Indian (V3)

The primary differentiator in global rollouts is the Year Shift. This is a displacement factor ($+1$, $0$, or $-1$) that aligns the calendar year with the financial year.

The Western Style (K4)

The fiscal year matches the calendar year.

  • January: Period 01 / Shift 0
  • December: Period 12 / Shift 0

The Indian Style (V3)

The Indian fiscal year starts in April. This creates a technical overlap that requires a displacement shift.

  • April to December: These months are part of the current calendar year. Year Shift = 0.
  • January to March: These months belong to the next calendar year but the current fiscal year. To bring them back into the current fiscal year's reporting, we apply a Year Shift = -1.

Consultant Trap: If you are configuring a new Indian Company Code and forget the $-1$ shift for Jan/Feb/March, the system will try to post a March 2026 invoice into Fiscal Year 2026 instead of 2025, throwing the entire Balance Sheet out of equilibrium.

  1. Case Study: Shortened Fiscal Year During Corporate Realignment

The Scenario:

An Indian subsidiary of a German conglomerate is migrating its legacy systems to the global SAP S/4HANA template. The Indian entity currently follows V3 (April–March), but the German parent company mandates K4 (January–December) for global consolidation starting January 1st, 2026.

The Solution: The "Transition Variant"

You cannot simply change the variant in OB37 mid-year; doing so would corrupt the Material Ledger history.

  1. Define the Shortened Year: In OB29, create a new Year-Dependent variant (e.g., Z9).
  2. Period Compression: For the transition year (2025), you define only 9 periods (April 2025 to December 2025).
  3. The Cutover: On December 31st, 2025, the "Year 2025" ends.
  4. The Re-assignment: In the new year, the Company Code is assigned to the standard K4 variant.

Critical MM Checklist for this Transition:

  • MMPV: Ensure the last period of the shortened year (Period 09) is closed correctly.
  • Purchase Orders: Any open POs with delivery dates in the "missing" months (Jan-March) must be reviewed to ensure the new period logic doesn't block the GR.
  • Physical Inventory: A full wall-to-wall count is recommended at the end of the shortened year to "cleanse" the valuation before the new cycle begins.
  1. Integration Points for the MM Consultant

While FI defines the variant, the MM consultant must manage the operational impact:

  • T-Code OMSY: This is where we see the link between the Company Code and the Fiscal Year. It shows the current, previous, and last period of the previous year.
  • T-Code MMPV: This is the "Period Closer." It checks against the FYV to move the "Current Period" forward. If your FYV says Period 12 ends on March 31, but you try to run MMPV on March 30, the system will prevent the shift.
  • Material Valuation: SAP stores material stocks and values by period. If the FYV is changed without a proper migration strategy, the historical stock tables (MARCH, MARDH) will become inconsistent, leading to severe audit failures.

Conclusion

The Fiscal Year Variant is the heartbeat of SAP. Whether you are managing a standard K4 setup or a complex Year-Dependent shortened year for a merger, the logic remains the same: align the calendar to the business cycle through the Year Shift.

entry.title: ECC + Belgium eDocument: Should we use AIF_PROXY or DRCC for SAP DRC Cloud?
entry.content:
Hello experts,

I am working on the Belgium eInvoicing integration using SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, cloud edition (SAP DRC Cloud) connected to SAP ECC through SAP Cloud Connector.

I have followed the official SAP documentation for:

  • Defining SOA Services for Communication (which explicitly mentions communication with the cloud edition of SAP DRC)
  • Defining Interface Types for eDocument
  • Defining Process Communication Through Cloud Services

However, I am unsure which Interface Type should be used for Belgium (BEINV):

1. AIF_PROXY (for web services)

This is the default interface type available in the system for Belgium and that's the value mentionned in the SAP documentation

When using AIF_PROXY, the eDocument is created but remains in “Created” or goes to error (red) and nothing is sent to SAP DRC Cloud.

2. DRCC (Cloud Edition: Standard Integration)

This option exists in the drop‑down list, and according to SAP documentation this should be the correct one for SAP DRC Cloud integration.

However, when selecting DRCC, I get this error:

EDOCUMENT122 – “Interface Type ‘Cloud Edition: Standard Integration’ not allowed”

Because of this error, I cannot assign DRCC to the Belgian eDocument type, and ECC continues to use the old AIF/SOA framework instead of the DRC Cloud interface.

What I have already checked:

  • Cloud Connector is working (green)
  • SOAMANAGER is configured
  • “Define Process Communication Through Cloud Services” is set to X (Cloud Service)
  • SOA Services are assigned
  • eDocument is generated in EDOC_COCKPIT
  • But SAP DRC Cloud never receives anything

My questions:

  1. For Belgium + SAP DRC Cloud, should we be using DRCC or AIF_PROXY as the Interface Type in ECC?
    SAP Help refers to AIF_PROXY

  2. Does the error EDOCUMENT122 mean that an activation, support package or country note is missing for Belgium DRC Cloud integration?

  3. Is there an additional add‑on or SAP Note required to enable DRCC for Belgium in ECC?

Any guidance, confirmation, or pointers to the correct documentation for Belgium eInvoicing via SAP DRC Cloud (not the classic AIF-based eDocument) would be extremely helpful.

Thank you!

entry.title: How to use fields of associated consumption view (not composition) in RAP root view's metadata ext.
entry.content:
Hi All,

  So here is the scenario.  

  I have 1) Table T1-> CDS ZI_T1 -> CDS ZC_T1 -> MetadataExtension (ZMDE_T1)

              2) Table T2-> CDS ZI_T2 -> CDS ZC_T2 -> MetadataExtension (ZMDE_T2)

Now T2 is not composition/child of T1 , but just an association. So we have ZI_T1 having association with ZI_T2 and exposed the association _ZT2.

Now for the List report UI in RAP , we have fields of ZC_T1 shown in List Page and I want when user goes to object page , it should show fields of _ZT2 in a table (UI.lineitem)

I have created Metadata extensions and facets on top of both the consumption views.

Challenge here is since my MDE of ZC_T2 will not be accessible in MDE of ZC_T1 to navigate during object page , since there is no direct association which exists between ZC_T1 and ZC_T2.

So i have to forcefully change my association  in ZI_T1 to point to ZC_T2 (instead of ZI_T2) and then the UI works in object page as expected.

But problem with this approach is , 1) its not correct as per SAP standard to associate an interface view with consumption view.

Also  2) its not recommended as per standards to create metadata extension on top of interface view (for T2) as it should always be consumption view.

Now I am unable to achieve my requirement without breaking one of the above coding standards.

Please suggest

entry.title: Error creating my dataset on SAP AI Core
entry.content:
Hi everyone,

I'm following the tutorial Ingest Live Data into your House Price Predictor with SAP AI Core and encountering an issue when trying to ingest my dataset from an AWS S3 bucket.

Error Description

My workflow execution fails with exit code 64 when attempting to save the trained model back to S3. The training completes successfully (I can see the model files are archived), but the workflow fails during the artifact upload phase.

Error Message:

Error (exit code 64): failed to put directory: The bucket you are attempting to access must be addressed using the specified endpoint. Please send all future requests to this endpoint.

My Configuration

Object Store Secret:

 
response = ai_core_client.object_store_secrets.create(
    name="mys3",
    path_prefix="datasets",
    type="S3",
    data={
        "AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID": "<MY_KEY>",
        "AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY": "<MY_SECRET>"
    },
    bucket="sap-aicore-house-price-data",
    region="us-east-2",
    endpoint="s3-us-east-2.amazonaws.com",  # Tried multiple formats
    resource_group="default"
)

I've tested multiple endpoint formats without success:

  • s3.us-east-2.amazonaws.com (with dot)
  • s3-us-east-2.amazonaws.com (with hyphen - as shown in tutorial)
  • s3.amazonaws.com (global endpoint)
  • No endpoint parameter (letting SAP AI Core resolve automatically)

Ravi

Ben

entry.content:
***English version below***

Unser nächster SAP Stammtisch Dortmund findet am 01. April 2026 ab 18:30 Uhr statt.

Eingeladen ist jeder, der Lust und Zeit hat – unabhängig vom fachlichen Hintergrund, von Entwicklern, Beratern, Projektmanagern, Basisadministratoren bis hin zu Endanwendern. Im Mittelpunkt steht ein lockerer fachlicher Austausch rund um SAP‑Themen über Unternehmensgrenzen hinweg.

Wir freuen uns auf bekannte und neue Gesichter. Melde dich idealerweise bis zum 25.03. an, damit wir besser planen können. Aber auch eine spontane Teilnahme ist herzlich willkommen.

Wo? HÖVELS Hausbrauerei, Hoher Wall 5–7, Dortmund, Germany

Bis dann!

---

Our next SAP Community Meetup Dortmund will take place on April 1st, 2026 at 6:30 PM.

Everyone is invited, regardless of professional background—from developers, consultants, and project managers to basis administrators and end users. The focus is on a relaxed professional exchange about SAP topics across company boundaries.

We look forward to seeing both familiar and new faces. Ideally, please RSVP by March 25th so we can plan accordingly. However, spontaneous participation is also welcome.

See you then!

entry.title: Re: How to connect S4 HANA On-prem to SAP Business Data C...

Hi Deb,

SAP BDC, managed data products and Intelligent applications are not available on the on-prem edition. SAP S/4HANA needs to be on Public or Private cloud. Hence you are unable to include SAP S/4HANA on-prem in formation.

https://help.sap.com/docs/business-data-cloud/administering-sap-business-data-cloud/creating-sap-business-data-cloud-formations

For Private Cloud SAP S/4HANA blog is available with the detailed steps:

https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/integrating-sap-business-data-cloud-s-4hana-cloud-private-edition/ba-p/14115767

Thank you,

Jeet

entry.title: Re: Obsolete Tcode/ new Tcode after upgrading SAP S/4HANA...
entry.content:
Hello @quocdt5 

there are some ways how to handle this task and depends in general on existing SAP Auth Concept.

The working one is to specify the scope - get roles content, e.g. by export of table AGR_1251,  auth object S_TCODE, field TCD, excl.  ones marked "Deleted". Then is needed to take the list of contained t-codes, remove dupes, save as .csv and upload into Fiori Apps Reference Library to run SAP Fiori Upgrade Impact Analysis. You will get 4 or 5 lists - Unavailable Apps, Deprecated Apps, Available Apps and Unknown Apps. Download them and e.g. using VLOOKUP in MS Excel you'll see how much effort is needed to adjust existing roles.

My fingers crossed to succeeed!

BR, Olga

entry.title: Re: SAP Process Integration Runtime Creation Failed
entry.content:
Hi Prabhakar,

The second error is derived from the first one, meaning that without the activation of the capabilities on the Integration Suite you cannot create the SAP Process Integration Runtime. 

I would suggest you add the following roles to your user to access Integration Suite:
PI_Administrator
PI_Business_Expert
PI_Integration_Developer
Check if the user you are trying to access Integration Suite has these roles. 
Hope it helps,
Doris

entry.title: SAP BI 2025 Patches: Web Intelligence and Semantic Layer
entry.content:
With the SAP BI 2025 release, a new maintenance model applies where patches are regularly released to fix defects. The patches released to date are:

  • SAP BI 2025 Patch 01: May 12, 2025
  • SAP BI 2025 Patch 02: June 6, 2025
  • SAP BI 2025 Patch 03: July 8, 2025
  • SAP BI 2025 Patch 04: August 8, 2025
  • SAP BI 2025 Patch 05: Sept 23, 2025
  • SAP BI 2025 Patch 06: Nov 11, 2025
  • SAP BI 2025 Patch 07: Dec 15, 2025
  • SAP BI 2025 Patch 08: Jan 23, 2026

For more details, refer to the SAP BI 2025 Patches Release Notes.

Until the SAP BI 2027 release, these patches may also deliver minor changes intended to complete existing functionality.

This article is part of the What’s New in Web Intelligence and Semantic Layer series and summarizes these changes for Web Intelligence and Semantic Layer:

  • SAP BI 2025 Patch 01
  • Web Intelligence: Purging Documents
  • Semantic Layer: Derived Tables in WITH Clause
  • Information Design Tool: Universe Conversion
  • SAP BI 2025 Patch 02
  • Web Intelligence: Sort Sections
  • Web Intelligence: Hide Free Elements and Merged Objects in Drill Filters
  • Web Intelligence: Insert an Icon
  • SAP BI 2025 Patch 06
  • Web Intelligence: Usability
  • Web Intelligence: Rich Text Cell
  • SAP BI 2025 Patch 07
  • Web Intelligence: Change Source Usability
  • Web Intelligence: Query Panel Usability
  • Web Intelligence: Set Relative Position
  • SAP BI 2025 Patch 08
  • Web Intelligence: SAP BI 4.3 SP3 Cubes Conversion

This article will be updated as new patches are released.

SAP BI 2025 Patch 01

Web Intelligence: Purging Documents

Purging a query removes also its results if they are used as filters in other queries through a filter of type "Result from another query".

For more details, refer to this SAP BI 4.3 SP5 article.

Semantic Layer: Derived Tables in WITH Clause

You can simplify the generated SQL for derived tables in relational universe. To do so, you must set the DERIVED_TABLES_AS_CTE parameter to Yes in the data foundation Query Script Parameters dialog box before publishing the universe.

For more details, refer to this SAP BI 4.3 SP5 article.

Information Design Tool: Universe Conversion

In SAP BI 2025, universes created with the Universe Design Tool are no longer supported by the Information Design Tool. The only official method to convert these UNV universes is by using the Semantic Layer Java SDK.

However, there is a workaround to enable conversion directly in the Information Design Tool :

  1. Open the InformationDesignTool.ini file located in the Information Design Tool installation folder.
  2. Add the following line at the end of the file:
    -Dsap.sl.showUNV=true
  3. Save the file and restart the Information Design Tool. You will then be able to select and convert UNV universes within the tool.

Back to Top ⤒

SAP BI 2025 Patch 02

Web Intelligence: Sort Sections

You can use any objects to sort section instances, not just the object used to create the section. This is useful, for example, when you want to sort a section by a measure, such as sorting cities by their number of inhabitants.

For more details, refer to this SAP BI 4.3 SP5 article.

Web Intelligence: Hide Free Elements and Merged Objects in Drill Filters

You can hide free drill elements and merged dimensions in the drill filters to prevent users from seeing these objects’ values when drilling in Reading mode.

For more details, refer to this SAP BI 4.3 SP5 article.

Web Intelligence: Insert an Icon

When inserting an icon from the Quick Access side panel > Report Elements tab, the icon picker opens in a compact pop-over. In this pop-over, you can:

  • Select an icon, then click on the report to place it.
  • Press the [Esc] key or click the Close icon () in the pop-over header to cancel this action.

Insert an Icon Pop-Over

When launched from the main toolbar, the icon picker opens as a dialog box.

In both cases, the Details view—previously used to display icon tags—has been removed.

Back to Top ⤒

SAP BI 2025 Patch 06

Web Intelligence: Usability

Several usability enhancements have been introduced to simplify document design:

  • In the Quick Access side panel > Data Sources tab, the expanded or collapsed state of the Recents section is now saved in the user settings.
  • In the Main side panel > Objects tab, when duplicating a variable, the focus automatically moves to the newly created variable.
  • For SAP BW and SAP HANA data sources, a checkbox has been added in the Set as prompt column header, allowing you to enable or disable this property for all variables at once.

Set Variables Dialog Box: Set as Prompt

Web Intelligence: Rich Text Cell

In Design mode, when editing a Rich Text cell in the Build > Format > Specific Settings tab:

  • In the text field toolbar, you can click the Insert an icon button () to open the icon picker. Select an icon to place it into the text.
  • The Text, Layout and Multiple Value Management sections can be expanded and collapsed.

Rich Text Settings

Back to Top ⤒

SAP BI 2025 Patch 07

Web Intelligence: Change Source Usability

In the Change Source dialog box:

  • When you select an existing query in the document as the target, a new column displaying the query name is added next to the one with data source name (Request 349866). The selected data source and query to change are displayed in bold.
  • The Apply changes in all queries sharing the same data source checkbox has been moved below the source data source. It is displayed only when applicable — that is, when the data source is used by multiple queries.

Change Source Dialog Box

Web Intelligence: Query Panel Usability

In the Query Panel, the Search filter has been replaced with a dynamic filter that updates data source objects on the fly.

Query Panel Objects Filter

Web Intelligence: Set Relative Position

In Design mode, a new shortcut makes it easier to position one report element relative to another. Instead of using the Build > Format > Layout tab to set relative positions, simply press the [Shift] key.

  • If no report element is selected, dotted arrows appear to indicate the relative positions of all report elements.

Display All Relative Positions

  • If one or more report elements are selected, only the relationships between those selected elements are displayed, with the referenced elements highlighted in blue.

Display Selected Elements Relative Positions

  • When moving a report element while holding the [Shift] key, arrows appear to show alignment with other elements. If you release the element while still holding [Shift], it will be positioned vertically and horizontally relative to the aligned elements.

Setting Relative Position

Negative relative position is not allowed. If you move a report element that is positioned relatively to another one:

  • If the position relative to this other report element remains positive, the frame of this element is highlighted in blue.
  • If the relative position becomes negative, the horizontal and/or vertical border of the frame turn(s) orange to warn that the affected relative positioning (horizontal, vertical, or both) will be disabled. Upon releasing the mouse:
    • The affected relative positioning is disabled if the position became invalid.
    • A toast message is displayed at the bottom of the window. This message replaces the former Warning/Confirmation dialog box.

Negative Relative Position

Back to Top ⤒

SAP BI 2025 Patch 08

Web Intelligence: SAP BI 4.3 SP3 Cubes Conversion

When Data mode was released in SAP BI 4.3 SP3, merging cubes (left join, append) was not supported and was possible only by enabling a toggle.

Such cubes created in SAP BI 4.3 SP3 are converted to the new supported format when the document in which they were created is opened.

Back to Top ⤒

entry.title: Re: Introduce profit center postings for subsequent activ...
entry.content:
Hello @ravi_dvg ,

From a best practice perspective, a profit center should be derived naturally from the material master, SD item, WBS item, or cost center rather than through FI substitution. While substitution can be used as a workaround, it introduces reconciliation and reporting risks during subsequent activation of Document Splitting. A consistent end-to-end derivation logic is recommended.  Any inconsistency in profit center derivation can cause splitting errors, balance inconsistencies, or migration issues during activation.
Try it in Quality first.

Vaibhav

entry.title: POV on How to Approach Custom Agents in the Enterprise - Blog Series
entry.content:
You've invested in AI agents across your enterprise - some from SAP, others from your broader tech stack. But as your AI ecosystem grows, so does a critical question: how do I approach my use cases strategically across this diverse landscape?

This blog addresses that question by addressing several POVs on how you can approach your AI agentic use cases both with SAP and non-SAP.

The POVs I will focus on are:

  1. POV 1- Creating Custom Agents in SAP
  2. POV 2 - Agent to Agent Collaboration with SAP and non-SAP Agents
  3. POV 3 - Using Integration Suite as Your Governed MCP Server Platform
  4. POV 4 - Putting it all Together Across Your Enterprise

Read BEFORE the POVs:

You first need to understand when you would require a custom agent as there is always additional governance and build effort associated with maintaining an agent’s lifecycle.

SAP recommends that custom agents or agent extensions should only be created when your prioritized use case does not already exist out-of-the-box (OOTB). How would you know if it exists OOTB? Easy! Our SAP Discovery Center contains an AI Catalog that you can filter by solution and/or business area to see what Agents and Embedded AI capabilities are available for out-of-the-box use.

Image taken from AI Catalog

If the agents meet partial or no requirements, then it is safe to say you can create a custom agent extension.

What will you get out of this Blog Series?

By the end of this blog, you'll understand how to approach your AI agent strategy - from leveraging existing SAP capabilities to building custom solutions that integrate and orchestrate collaboration between SAP and non-SAP agents across your enterprise landscape.

Click to begin reading POV 1 - Creating Custom Agents in SAP.

entry.title: POV 1: Create Custom Agents in SAP (Part 1/4)
entry.content:
This Blog is Part 1 of a Blog Series (4 Parts!). Click on the following to navigate back to the main menu of POV on How to Approach Custom Agents in the Enterprise - Blog Series.

POV 1: Create Custom Agents in SAP

If your business requirements cannot be fulfilled with the out-of-the-box SAP Joule Agents and you wish to use SAP data for your custom agent, then SAP Build's Joule Studio Agent Builder is the recommended solution.

In Joule Studio Agent Builder, you can use no-code/low-code as well as natural language prompting (through embedded Vibe Coding) to create custom Joule skills and Joule Agents. As a reminder, Joule skills are deterministic meaning they will be able to complete a specific task relatively quickly. Joule agents, on the other hand, reason and problem solve across multi-step scenarios within a defined business domain.

Image taken from Joule Studio Agent Builder; showcasing embedded Vibe Coding used to create a custom Restocking Agent. Source

Why would you want to create SAP agents within SAP?

  1. SAP knows SAP best - Joule is the agent orchestrator that allows it to be proactive and is deeply connected to business context and deep domain knowledge. SAP's business context is the "magic" of joule that helps determine the sequence and which agents or tools to use. SAP Agents will always have the business context to understand what is being asked of them and how to answer, therefore resulting in more reliable and relevant responses.
  2. Controlled Enterprise-wide Governance - Joule is strictly role based. Meaning it is tightly integrated across the SAP security landscape through BTP's Cloud Identity Service (CIS) - which manages user authentication and access between your apps and Joule. For example, if Joe with US specific Finance roles & authorizations (R&A) is given access to a custom agent containing world-wide Finance info, Joe will only see financial data/information relevant to the US as per his assigned R&A. Similarly, Joe will be able to prompt Joule regardless of the SAP cloud application, if he has access. For example, if he has access to S/4HANA Cloud, SuccessFactors, and Work Zone - he will be able to use any app’s Joule interface to prompt it. Joule’s security landscape results in responsible responses across the SAP landscape. Please see the Architecture Diagram for detailed architecture of Joule Studio and CIS.
  3. Connect non-SAP data - Joule allows you to connect non-SAP data sources using APIs, as well as grounded external/internal documents allowing you to blend non-SAP and SAP data with your grounded policies and documentation.

In short, creating an SAP Agent outside of SAP will result in additional effort to rebuild business context and security.

Next POV?

To check out Part 2 of the Blog Series click: POV 2 - Agent to Agent Collaboration with SAP and non-SAP Agents.

Or click on the following to navigate back to the main menu of POV on How to Approach Custom Agents in the Enterprise - Blog Series.

 

Source: Blog by Michael on Joule Agent Builder

Source: Blog by Amit Bangora on Joule Skill Build

entry.title: POV 2: Agent to Agent Collaboration with SAP and non-SAP Agents (Part 2/4)
entry.content:
This Blog is Part 2 of a Blog Series (4 Parts!). Click on the following to navigate back to the main menu of POV on How to Approach Custom Agents in the Enterprise - Blog Series.

POV 2: Agent to Agent Collaboration with SAP and non-SAP Agents 

*A2A Interoperability Coming Soon in 2026

In some cases, you may want to leverage your non-SAP Agents with SAP agents. As discussed in POV 1, bringing SAP data outside of SAP will result in challenges in security, governance, and business context - compromising the agent’s responsibility, relevance, and reliability.

This is a great opportunity to combine the best of both worlds - SAP & non-SAP Agents through Agent-to-Agent collaboration.

How? The A2A open protocol, developed by Google, handles inter-agent communication allowing agents, regardless of their vendor, to communicate complex tasks. Many of you may have already heard of MCP (Model Context Protocol), however if you would like a refresh – check out this blog by Pramod. It is important to note that A2A does not replace MCP but rather works alongside it. Think of A2A as the communication handler between agents where one agent assigns a subtask to another and the MCP enables the agents with the right tooling to complete the task. Using A2A with MCP enables autonomous AI agents regardless of if they are SAP or non-SAP.

From SAP the key orchestrator of agent collaboration is Joule, as discussed in POV 1, where it orchestrates the sequencing of tasks and workflows using deep domain knowledge and business context. What does this mean in terms of what autonomous agent-to-agent collaboration can achieve between SAP and non-SAP?

  1. Agents are Discoverable - AI Agents will have discoverable endpoints that will be captured in the SAP AI Agent catalog acting as an adapter between SAP and non-SAP agents. As per the diagram below there will be an auto-assigned ORD (capture agent metadata) to ensure the Agent is discoverable. Through this, Joule will know what the agents can do to ensure the right one is selected to complete a task. (see architecture diagram below)
  2. Delegate tasks/artifacts - There are 2 types of agents to facilitate A2A communication: a client agent and remote agents where the client agent sends the task (eg. prompt or system triggered) and the remote agent receives the task, matches the tools, and executes the task. A2A is designed to be task-oriented and with the combination with its discoverable endpoints and deep domain knowledge, business context, Joule will understand how to orchestrate internally and with external agents.
  3. Operate securely and interoperable across systems - As per the diagram below, all external agents will be routed through the Agent Catalog making them discoverable and preventing direct exposure of SAP Agents to external agents. In addition, Agents will have a secure tool layer (Tool catalogue) where agents will have access to tools within trust boundaries that respect security policies.
  4. Scale your enterprise – rather than operating agents in silos, you can now have a connected interoperable agentic ecosystem with distributed functionality/tasks making it easier to scale.

The above image shows A2A Architecture with SAP. For example, a customer service rep receives a billing inquiry in their email. The service rep initiates a dispute resolution process through Joule and engages with a Google Agent (e.g. BigQuery data Source) that retrieves the required data and shares it back to Joule - collaborating to generate insights and resolution recommendations. To see this lab preview A2A scenario in action, check out this github.

Therefore, you can leverage all your investments, SAP & non-SAP, to have a connected Agent ecosystem.

Next POV?

To check out Part 3 of the Blog Series click: POV 3 - Using Integration Suite as Your Governed MCP Server Platform.

Or click on the following to navigate back to the main menu of POV on How to Approach Custom Agents in the Enterprise - Blog Series.

 

Source: A2A Blog by Google

Source: SAP A2A Architecture

entry.title: POV 3: Using Integration Suite as Your Governed MCP Server Platform (Part 3/4)
entry.content:
This Blog is Part 3 of a Blog series (4 Parts!). Click on the following to navigate back to the main menu of POV on How to Approach Custom Agents in the Enterprise - Blog Series.

 POV 3: Using Integration Suite as Your Governed MCP Server Platform *Coming Q2/2026

If you didn't have a chance to tune in to SAP Tech Ed's IN200v | Empower your agents to be responsible (2025) then it is notable that in Q2/2026 SAP Integration Suite's API Management will allow you to apply additional security policies to APIs in your MCP servers and publish your secure APIs with MCP documentation for Agent consumption via the API and MCP Gateway.

Why is this important?

MCP allows agents to access external information for systems, APIs, documents all while reducing agent complexity. In summary, agents with a MCP client will rely on the MCP server to provide structured metadata to the APIs/Protocols. However, these APIs within the MCP server will likely not meet all security requirements and pose some security risk.

With SAP Integration Suite's API and MCP gateway, you will be able to apply additional API Policies around multi-protocol connectivity, routing, transformations (eg. JSON to XML), mapping, traffic management, and security requirements to further secure, monitor and govern your APIs being leveraged as part of your MCP. This will be incredibly powerful for both SAP Agents and non-SAP agents.

Image taken from SAP Tech Ed's IN200v | Empower your agents to be responsible (2025)

How is this relevant to on-premise users?

Users that are on-premise (eg. ECC, S/4HANA on-prem) can leverage Integration Suite to create custom APIs through OData Services or modify prebuilt S/4HANA APIs (see Business Accelerator Hub) and apply additional API policies to reduce security risk. These APIs can then be leveraged in SAP as well as non-SAP agents. However, it is important to consider the rebuild effort of business context and security when accessing SAP data outside of Joule agents (as described in POV 1).

Where else can Integration Suite and SAP help in my Agent Build?

SAP Business Data Graphs! Within Integration Suite you can explore, design, and extend Graph APIs (Odata V4 or GraphQL) for agent consumption.

Think of this as a semantic layer (connecting multiple entities) that sits on top of the API that allows your agent to receive a mix of entities from one central entity - removing the Agent’s effort to combine the entities together.

Graphs are powerful because the projections between data sources (entities) are automatically created between SAP data as SAP leverages the One Domain data model. However, there are also capabilities for developers to extend to non-SAP. Therefore, allowing you to have a simplified semantically rich API ready for Agent consumption with the bonus to apply additional API policies on top of it, resulting in a secure Graph API. For more information on Graph APIs please check out this blog on an Intro to SAP Graphs & Graph in Integration Suite Blog.

An example of this can be seen in the image below that summarizes the demo shown at SAP Tech Ed's IN200v | Empower your agents to be responsible session. You can see that a user prompts an Agent that leverages MCP API tools with additional governance policies applied to the Graph API. This showcases secure and semantically connected data across SAP Risk assessment, Supplier Plant, and Supplier plant materials entities - resulting in higher performing responsible, relevant and reliable AI Agent response.

 

 

 

Source: Graph – SAP Help

Source: Watch Tech Ed sessions on-demand

 

Next POV?

To check out Part 4 of the Blog Series click: POV 4 - Putting it all Together Across Your Enterprise.

Or click on the following to navigate back to the main menu of POV on How to Approach Custom Agents in the Enterprise - Blog Series.

entry.title: POV 4: Putting it all Together Across Your Enterprise (Part 4/4)
entry.content:
This Blog is Part 4 of a Blog Series (4 Parts!). Click on the following to navigate back to the main menu of POV on How to Approach Custom Agents in the Enterprise - Blog Series.

 POV 4: Putting it All Together Across Your Enterprise

Keeping in mind POV 1, 2, and 3 - POV 4 focuses on bringing your enterprise-wide agents together with an additional layer of security and governance.

As discussed, Integration Suite will allow you to create custom APIs - SAP and non-SAP- including Business Data Graph APIs. These can then be further consumed, whether it’s a custom API through SAP or non-SAP or an API that’s part of a MCP server. In other words, both Joule and non-SAP agents will be able to leverage secure governed APIs and MCPs in an interoperable agentic ecosystem.

In simpler words: Integration Suite and Joule Studio will be the key to unlocking secure, governed, and business context rich agent ecosystems.

 

In Summary

We opened with a critical question: How do you approach your use cases strategically across your diverse AI landscape? The answer lies in three integrated capabilities:

  1. SAP Joule provides the foundation for responsible, reliable and relevant custom agents with Joule through its deep domain knowledge, rich business context, and secure governance capabilities.
  2. Joule can be used for both SAP specific use cases as well as non-SAP Agent collaboration - bringing the best of both worlds together.
  3. Finally, Integration Suite's API management and MCP Gateway capabilities will be key to unlocking an additional powerful security and governance layer for your agents across your enterprise.

 

This concludes the Blog Series: POV on How to Approach Custom Agents in the Enterprise.

entry.title: Requirement Date in Subcontracting Component SAP S4 HANA CLOUD PUBLIC EDITION
entry.content:
Hello experts.

I have a question about why the system is using a date from 4 days ago as the Requirement Date in my Subcontracting Components?

I have understood and verified that my delivery date is calculated from my info record, as you can see.

 

 

 

 

But if we look at the subcontract component, the system indicates a Requirement Date  4 days ago, why is this happening?

In the master data of my component I do not have any planned delivery time I have deleted and created an info record for 17 days and the system still calculates 4 days ago.

 

But if I manually move the delivery date, the system automatically adjusts it by approximately 7 days.
I've read note 2176766 - Requirement date (re)calculation logic in subcontracting, but I don't understand the calculation logic.
I look forward to your comments.
Thank you very much.
